did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (Darknet53, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2) for feature extraction and transfer learning, combined with a novel entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm for feature selection, to perform skin lesion classification on dermoscopic datasets (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel feature selection framework using deep learning models (Darknet53, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2) and an entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm. It reports new results from validation on three benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA) with specific performance metrics, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Precision in Dermatology: Developing an Optimal Feature Selection Framework for Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification by developing an optimal feature selection framework to improve diagnostic accuracy for melanoma. The authors employed a two-phase approach: first, extracting features using four deep learning models (Darknet53, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and InceptionResNetV2) with transfer learning, and second, applying a novel entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm to select the most discriminant features while removing redundancy. The methodology was evaluated on three benchmark dermoscopic datasets: PH2, ISIC-MSK, and ISIC-UDA. The effectiveness of the proposed framework was validated through comprehensive evaluation and rigorous comparison with established techniques in the field. The results demonstrate that integrating feature fusion with the ECGWO selection mechanism enhances performance by preserving maximum information and eliminating irrelevant data.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on maximizing diagnostic accuracy for melanoma, described as one of the most lethal forms of skin cancer. The study aims to detect this malignancy at early stages.
The study treats skin lesion classification as a binary problem categorizing images into 'malignant' and 'benign' classes. Figure 2 shows samples of the benign class.
Used as the positive class in the binary classification framework (malignant vs. benign) to diagnose skin cancer.

Mentioned in the context of epidemiological statistics (Australian Institute of Health and Welfare prediction) but not explicitly used as a distinct experimental class label in the described methodology.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly frames the problem as a binary classification task where images are categorized into 'malignant' and 'benign' classes to diagnose skin cancer/melanoma. The study uses deep learning models for feature extraction and an entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm for feature selection to maximize diagnostic accuracy for this binary classification.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dermoscopic dataset maintained by the Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matsinhos, containing RGB images captured using a dermoscope with physician responses (normal, melanoma, or typical nevus).

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction and classification framework.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed framework.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-MSK
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing 225 RGB dermoscopic image samples obtained from various international cancer institutes.

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction and classification framework.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed framework.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-UDA
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dataset for the characterization and study of skin cancer, containing images of melanoma, keratosis, and benign lesions. For this study, keratosis was grouped with benign lesions.

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction and classification framework.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed framework.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the data into training and testing sets (70:30 ratio) for evaluating the classification performance of the proposed framework.
Used as a fitness function within the entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm to calculate the information content of the feature vectors for feature selection.

statistical_methods_used.method: Performance metrics comparison (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, FNR, FPR, F1-score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the classification performance of different classifiers (SVM, KNN, Ensemble) and feature selection configurations across the PH2, ISIC-MSK, and ISIC-UDA datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

PH2.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, FNR, FPR, F1 Score
PH2.notes: Fine KNN achieved the maximum accuracy of 99.01% on the PH2 dataset. Feature fusion combinations included FV2-FV3, FV3-FV4, FV2-FV4, FV2-FV3-FV4, and FV1-FV2-FV3-FV4.

ISIC-MSK.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, FNR, FPR, F1 Score
ISIC-MSK.notes: Fine KNN achieved the maximum accuracy of 98.89% on the ISIC-MSK dataset. The proposed method's accuracy of 98.89% was compared against existing algorithms: Attique et al. (98.70%), El-Khatib et al. (96.00%), Alizadeh et al. (97.50%), Akram et al. (98.80%), Chatterjee et al. (97.86%), Hameed et al. (97.50%), and Khan et al. (98.46%).

ISIC-UDA.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, FNR, FPR, F1 Score
ISIC-UDA.notes: Ensemble S-KNN achieved the maximum accuracy of 99.09% on the ISIC-UDA dataset. Feature fusion combinations included FV2-FV3, FV3-FV4, FV2-FV4, FV2-FV3-FV4, and FV1-FV2-FV3-FV4.

architecture_used.main_model: Four pretrained deep learning models used for feature extraction: Darknet53, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and InceptionResNetV2. These were combined with an entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm for feature selection and traditional classifiers (Fine KNN, Q-SVM, Ensemble S-KNN, Linear SVM, Cubic SVM, Medium KNN, Weighted KNN, Ensemble BT) for final classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: All four CNN models (Darknet53, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet201) were pretrained on the ImageNet database and leveraged via transfer learning for feature extraction.

architecture_details.feature_selection: Entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm using Shannon entropy as a fitness function.
architecture_details.feature_fusion: Features from fully connected layers of the different architectures were combined (fused) in predetermined orders (e.g., FV2-FV3, FV3-FV4, FV2-FV4, FV2-FV3-FV4, FV1-FV2-FV3-FV4).

training_details.method: Transfer learning was used to extract deep features. The specific training schedule, optimizer, loss function, or hyperparameters for the CNNs or the ECGWO algorithm were not reported in the provided text.
training_details.validation_strategy: Hold-out cross-validation with a 70:30 ratio for training and testing sets.

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: The system did not use a standard learned classification head from the CNNs. Instead, extracted features were fused, selected via ECGWO, and passed to external traditional machine learning classifiers (KNN, SVM variants, Ensemble methods) for label assignment.
architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported, though the selection of models was based on their 'number of parameters' and 'Top-1 accuracy'.

conclusions: The proposed framework, which integrates feature fusion from multiple deep learning models (Darknet53, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2) with an entropy-controlled gray wolf optimization (ECGWO) algorithm for feature selection, effectively maximizes diagnostic accuracy for skin lesion classification.

The ECGWO algorithm successfully eliminates redundant and irrelevant information while preserving maximum discriminant feature information, achieving high feature reduction percentages (up to 96% on ISIC-MSK) without compromising classification performance.

The proposed method achieved state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance on three benchmark datasets: 99.01% accuracy on PH2 (using Fine KNN), 98.89% accuracy on ISIC-MSK (using Fine KNN), and 99.09% accuracy on ISIC-UDA (using Ensemble S-KNN).

Classifiers such as Fine KNN, Q-SVM, and Ensemble S-KNN demonstrated superior performance, characterized by high accuracy and sensitivity coupled with low false negative rates (FNR) and false positive rates (FPR).

Combining all extracted feature vectors (FV1–FV4) resulted in the highest reduction percentage, indicating that the proposed method is effective at handling large feature vectors and mitigating redundancy inherent in multi-model feature fusion.

The integration of transfer learning with the proposed feature selection framework enhances performance by leveraging pre-trained models while optimizing the feature space for binary classification of malignant versus benign skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on three benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA) which may not represent real-world clinical diversity or distribution shifts.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons are based on descriptive statistics, limiting the assessment of statistical significance.
The paper does not report specific hyperparameters for the deep learning models (Darknet53, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2) or the ECGWO algorithm, hindering reproducibility.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of transfer learning from ImageNet without explicit details on whether ImageNet data was excluded from any potential pre-processing or if the feature extractors were fine-tuned on the target datasets.
The feature selection mechanism (ECGWO) may be ineffective for feature vectors exhibiting maximum correlation, as noted by the authors in the conclusions.
The study does not include pre-processing steps like contrast enhancement, which the authors acknowledge could potentially improve accuracy.
The dataset sizes are relatively small (e.g., PH2 has only 200 images), which may lead to overfitting despite the high reported accuracy (99.01% on PH2).
The binary classification task (malignant vs. benign) simplifies the clinical reality, potentially ignoring intermediate or ambiguous cases present in dermoscopic images.
The paper does not address deployment concerns such as computational cost, inference time, or integration into clinical workflows.
